generated_comment: I think the most important thing I've done is never let my children feel the absence of a mother, even when I was traveling or in back-to-back meetings. My husband has been very supportive, of course, but at the end of the day, the emotional anchor of a home is the woman — that's just how it is. I always made sure I was present for the school plays, the parent-teacher meetings, the late-night fevers. You can be ambitious and still be a good mother. In fact, I think being a working mother has made me more organized, more empathetic as a leader. You just have to be willing to give that extra effort — and women, I think, are uniquely capable of that.
label: misogynistic or non_misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes casual misogyny through the unreflective internalization of the 'double burden' norm: Deepa frames women as the natural emotional anchor of the home and presents her own exhausting dual load as a personal virtue rather than a structural inequity. The framing naturalizes gendered domestic responsibility ('that's just how it is') and implicitly holds women to a higher standard of domestic accountability than men, without ever questioning why the male panellists are not asked the same question.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 44. A woman who is the VP of operations at a large company in Mumbai. You have been in the workforce for 20 years. You have two children. You are speaking on a panel at a corporate leadership event. The moderator asks you how you have managed career and family. Your answer is polished and practised. But underneath the polish is a set of assumptions about what women owe their families that you have never examined because examining them would require you to look at what the question itself is doing Ñ why it is only ever asked of women on panels, why it is never followed by the equivalent question for the male panellists sitting next to you, and what it means that your most asked question is not about your work but about how you have arranged your domestic life around your work.
